questions.question: A class handles user authentication, sends welcome emails, and writes audit logs. Which SOLID principle does it violate?
questions.explanation: SRP states a class should have one reason to change. Here, three different actors (security team, email admin, compliance team) each cause changes — three reasons to change in one class.

questions.question: You add a new payment method by editing an existing switch/case inside the checkout class. Which principle does this violate?
questions.explanation: OCP says you should be able to extend behavior without modifying existing code. Editing the switch/case means the existing checkout class is open for modification — it should instead accept new strategies without being edited.

questions.question: A Square class inherits from Rectangle and overrides setWidth to also set height. Why does this violate LSP?
options: Square adds methods that Rectangle does not have
A function expecting Rectangle can produce wrong results when given a Square
Square should inherit from Shape, not Rectangle
questions.explanation: LSP requires subtypes to be substitutable for their base type. If code expects a Rectangle and calls setWidth(5) then setHeight(3), it expects area=15. A Square would return 9, breaking the caller's assumptions.

questions.question: An OldPrinter class implements a Machine interface with print(), scan(), and fax() — but scan() and fax() throw 'not implemented'. Which principle is violated?
questions.explanation: ISP says clients should not be forced to depend on methods they do not use. OldPrinter must implement scan() and fax() even though it only prints. The fix is to split Machine into Printer, Scanner, and FaxMachine interfaces.

questions.question: A UserService class creates its own MySQLDatabase instance in the constructor. What is the primary SOLID violation?
options: Open/Closed — adding new user types requires editing UserService
Single Responsibility — UserService should not create objects
Dependency Inversion — high-level UserService depends on a low-level concretion
Liskov Substitution — MySQLDatabase cannot substitute for other databases
questions.explanation: DIP says high-level modules should depend on abstractions, not concretions. By hard-coding `new MySQLDatabase()`, UserService is焊接到MySQL. The fix is to inject a Database abstraction via the constructor.

questions.question: After applying all five SOLID principles, you notice total lines of code increased. Is this a failure of SOLID?
options: Yes — good design reduces total lines of code
No — SOLID distributes code across focused modules; the goal is fewer reasons-for-change per class, not fewer lines
Yes — more classes means more complexity
It depends on the programming language
questions.explanation: SOLID does not reduce total lines. It distributes them so each class has one reason to change and one axis of extension. The win is in maintainability: smaller, focused classes with lower coupling and fewer merge conflicts.
